content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 20 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 110v–110r (LC PDF pp. 217–218) | **Source Images:** DEU_020_page_001.jpg, DEU_020_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 155–156 (pa_0155–pa_0156)

---

**1** When you go out to battle against your enemies and see horses and chariots and an army larger than yours, you shall not be afraid of them, for the LORD your God is with you, who brought you up from the land of Egypt. **2** And when you draw near to the battle, the priest shall approach and speak to the people **3** and shall say to them, "Hear, O Israel! Today you are drawing near to battle against your enemies. Let not your heart faint; do not be afraid or tremble or be in dread of them, **4** for the LORD your God is he who goes with you to fight for you against your enemies, to give you victory."

**5** Then the officers shall speak to the people, saying, "Is there any man who has built a new house and has not dedicated it? Let him go back to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man dedicate it. **6** Is there any man who has planted a vineyard and has not enjoyed its fruit? Let him go back to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man enjoy its fruit. **7** Is there any man who has betrothed a woman and has not married her? Let him go back to his house, lest he die in the battle and another man marry her." **8** And the officers shall speak further to the people and say, "Is there any man who is fearful and fainthearted? Let him go back to his house, lest the heart of his brothers faint like his heart." **9** And when the officers have finished speaking to the people, they shall appoint commanders of armies at the head of the people.

**10** When you draw near to a city to fight against it, you shall offer it terms of peace. **11** And if it responds to you peaceably and opens to you, then all the people who are found in it shall do forced labor for you and shall serve you. **12** But if it makes no peace with you, but makes war against you, then you shall besiege it. **13** And when the LORD your God gives it into your hand, you shall strike all its males with the edge of the sword. **14** But the women and the little ones, the livestock, and everything in the city, all its spoil, you shall take as plunder for yourself. And you shall enjoy the spoil of your enemies, which the LORD your God has given you. **15** Thus you shall do to all the cities that are very far from you, which are not cities of these nations here.

**16** But in the cities of these peoples that the LORD your God is giving you as an inheritance, you shall save alive nothing that breathes, **17** but you shall devote them to destruction—the Hittites and the Amorites, the Canaanites and the Perizzites, the Hivites and the Jebusites—as the LORD your God has commanded you, **18** that they may not teach you to do according to all their abominable practices that they have done for their gods, and so you would sin against the LORD your God.

**19** When you besiege a city for a long time, making war against it in order to capture it, you shall not destroy its trees by wielding an axe against them. You may eat from them, but you shall not cut them down. For is the tree of the field a man, that it should be besieged by you? **20** Only the trees that you know are not trees for food you may destroy and cut down, so that you may build siege works against the city that makes war with you, until it falls.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Formal Equivalence Analysis, Deuteronomy 20**

**v.1 "horses and chariots and an army larger than yours" (sus ve-rekev ve-am rav mimekha):** Lit. "horse and chariot and people more numerous than you." The triad horse-chariot-people represents the military might of great empires (Egypt, Canaan). Rendered with natural English plurals.

**v.2 "the priest shall approach" (nig'ash ha-kohen):** The kohen here is the anointed war-priest (kohen mashuakh la-milchamah, cf. 20:2 context). LXX: ὁ ἱερεύς, simply "the priest." The war-priest role is embedded in v.2's context without explicit title here.

**v.3 "Let not your heart faint" (al yirakh levavchem):** Lit. "let not your heart be soft/tender." The idiom for cowardice. LXX: μὴ ἐκλυέσθω ἡ καρδία ὑμῶν ("let not your heart be loosened"), a different metaphor for the same concept.

**v.3 "tremble or be in dread" (ve-al tirazu ve-al tachpazu):** Two near-synonyms for panic. "Tremble" (ruz) and "be in dread" (chaphaz) together form a merism of terror. LXX renders these with similar paired terms.

**v.5 "dedicated it" (chanakho):** The verb chanak means to dedicate/inaugurate a house with a ceremony. LXX: ἐνεκαίνισεν ("renewed/inaugurated"). LOGOS retains "dedicated" as the most natural English equivalent for the house-dedication ceremony.

**v.6 "enjoyed its fruit" (chilelo):** The Piel of chalal here means "to make common/profane," used technically of releasing a vineyard from its first-three-year prohibition (Lev 19:23-25). In practical terms, the owner "has not yet eaten its fruit." LOGOS: "enjoyed its fruit" captures the law's intention while preserving readability. LXX: εὐφράνθη ἐξ αὐτοῦ ("rejoiced from it").

**v.9 "commanders of armies" (sarey tzeva'ot):** Lit. "commanders/princes of armies." The military appointment language is kept formal.

**v.10 "offer it terms of peace" (qara'ta eleyha le-shalom):** Lit. "call to it for peace." The formal offer of surrender terms. LXX: εἰρηνικά ("peace terms"), matching the Hebrew concept.

**v.11 "forced labor" (mas):** The technical term for corvée labor. LXX: φόρους ("tribute"), which shifts the concept slightly from labor to taxation. MT's mas is labor-service specifically; "forced labor" is preferred.

**v.13 "with the edge of the sword" (le-phi cherev):** Lit. "to the mouth of the sword." The standard idiom for total combat killing. Rendered conventionally.

**v.16 "nothing that breathes" (kol neshamah):** Lit. "every breath/soul." The totality formula for the cherem ban. LXX: πᾶν ἐμπνέον ("everything breathing"), matching MT.

**v.17 "devote them to destruction" (tacharem otam):** The Hiphil of charam = to place under the ban, devote to destruction. "Devote to destruction" is the established formal rendering, preserving the sacral character of the act. LXX: ἀναθέματι ἀναθεματιεῖς ("with a ban you shall ban them"), using cognate constructions.

**v.19 "For is the tree of the field a man?" (ki ha-adam ets ha-sadeh lavo mipanekha ba-matsor?):** This difficult rhetorical question has been variously read. The most natural reading: "Is the tree of the field a human being, that you should besiege it?" The tree is not an enemy combatant; it should not be treated as one. LXX reads differently, understanding the clause as: "Is it a human being? No, it is a tree of the field." LOGOS follows the MT rhetorical question construction. The prohibition is grounded in natural law and practical humanity.

**v.20 "siege works" (matzor):** The noun form related to "siege." Rendered as "siege works" (earthworks, ramps, towers). LXX: πολιορκίαν, matching the concept.

— LOGOS
